title: The Beatitudes: Kingdom Values
objective: Understand the Beatitudes as Jesus' declaration of the character and blessings of those who belong to the kingdom of heaven

required_passages.text: Seeing the crowds, he went up on the mountain, and when he sat down, his disciples came to him. And he opened his mouth and taught them, saying: 'Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted. Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied. Blessed are the merciful, for they shall receive mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God. Blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness' sake, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are you when others revile you and persecute you and utter all kinds of evil against you falsely on my account. Rejoice and be glad, for your reward is great in heaven, for so they persecuted the prophets who were before you. You are the salt of the earth, but if salt has lost its taste, how shall its saltiness be restored? It is no longer good for anything except to be thrown out and trampled under people's feet. You are the light of the world. A city set on a hill cannot be hidden. Nor do people light a lamp and put it under a basket, but on a stand, and it gives light to all in the house. In the same way, let your light shine before others, so that they may see your good works and give glory to your Father who is in heaven.'

segments.audio_script: Welcome to lesson six. In lesson five Jesus called his first disciples and began teaching and healing across Galilee. Now Matthew takes us to a hillside where Jesus sits down — the posture of a rabbi ready to teach — and delivers what will become the most famous sermon in history. Today we focus on its opening movement: the Beatitudes. Eight declarations about who is truly blessed, followed by two vivid images of what kingdom people are meant to be in the world.

Matthew chapter 5, verses 1 through 16. Seeing the crowds, he went up on the mountain, and when he sat down, his disciples came to him. And he opened his mouth and taught them, saying: Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted. Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied. Blessed are the merciful, for they shall receive mercy. Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God. Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God. Blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness' sake, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are you when others revile you and persecute you and utter all kinds of evil against you falsely on my account. Rejoice and be glad, for your reward is great in heaven, for so they persecuted the prophets who were before you. You are the salt of the earth, but if salt has lost its taste, how shall its saltiness be restored? It is no longer good for anything except to be thrown out and trampled under people's feet. You are the light of the world. A city set on a hill cannot be hidden. Nor do people light a lamp and put it under a basket, but on a stand, and it gives light to all in the house. In the same way, let your light shine before others, so that they may see your good works and give glory to your Father who is in heaven.

Jesus ascending a mountain to deliver authoritative teaching would immediately recall Moses receiving the Law on Sinai. Matthew invites this comparison deliberately. But where Moses received law written on stone tablets, Jesus teaches from his own authority on the hillside. The word blessed in Greek is makarios. It does not describe an emotional state or a feeling of happiness. It describes the objective condition of someone who stands in God's favour — someone who is flourishing under his care regardless of outward circumstances. The Beatitudes are not instructions on how to become blessed. They are declarations describing people who already belong to the kingdom.

Notice the structure. The first and eighth beatitudes both end with the same promise: theirs is the kingdom of heaven. This brackets the entire list and suggests that all eight describe different facets of the same kingdom character. Each beatitude pairs a present condition with a future or present promise. The poor in spirit receive the kingdom now. The mourners will be comforted. The meek will inherit the earth. The pattern moves between present possession and future hope, anchoring disciples in both realities at once. The final beatitude expands significantly into the second person — blessed are you — and introduces persecution as the mark of authentic kingdom living. Jesus then pivots to salt and light. These are not commands to try harder. They are identity statements: you are salt, you are light. The calling follows from who they are, not the other way around.

The central theme is the inversion of worldly values. The world considers the powerful, the self-sufficient, and the comfortable to be blessed. Jesus declares the opposite. Those who know their own spiritual poverty, those who grieve over sin and suffering, those who make peace rather than war — these are the ones flourishing under God's reign. A second theme is the communal visibility of the kingdom. Salt and light both function by affecting what is around them. Kingdom people are not meant to exist in isolation. Their presence in the world — their good works, their integrity, their compassion — is what draws attention to the Father.
Review question: Which two beatitudes share the identical promise, and what is that promise? Answer: The first and the eighth beatitudes both carry the promise that theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
segments.correct_answer: The first and the eighth; theirs is the kingdom of heaven
acceptable_variants: poor in spirit and persecuted for righteousness; theirs is the kingdom of heaven
the first and last beatitudes both say theirs is the kingdom of heaven
beatitudes one and eight share the same promise: the kingdom of heaven
the first and eighth both promise the kingdom of heaven

segments.audio_script: You have completed lesson six. Jesus has declared what kingdom character looks like from the inside and what kingdom influence looks like from the outside. In lesson seven we continue through the Sermon on the Mount as Jesus addresses the Law itself — not to set it aside but to reveal its full and searching depth.

vocabulary.definition: The Greek word translated 'blessed' in the Beatitudes; refers not to an emotional feeling but to the objective state of being in God's favour and flourishing under his care
Those who recognise their own spiritual bankruptcy before God — the opposite of self-sufficiency — and therefore look entirely to him for what they need
